text_blocks.text: The Treaty of Hubertusburg,
between Austria, Prussia, and Saxony, was
signed on February 15, 1763, at a hunting
lodge between Dresden and Leipzig. Negotiations
had started there on December 31, 1762.
Frederick, who had considered ceding East
Prussia to Russia if Peter III helped him
secure Saxony, finally insisted on excluding
Russia (in fact, no longer a belligerent) from
the negotiations. At the same time, he
refused to evacuate Saxony until its elector
had renounced any claim to reparation. The
Austrians wanted at least to retain Glatz,
which they had in fact reconquered, but
Frederick would not allow it. The treaty
simply restored the status quo of 1748, with
Silesia and Glatz reverting to Frederick and
Saxony to its own elector. The only
concession that Prussia made to Austria was
to consent to the election of Archduke
Joseph as Holy Roman emperor.
